message.content:
You are auditing ONE documentation file in a ROS2 robotics project (TurtleBot 4 autonomous "guide robot").
Read these files: /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/docs/aws-iot/iot-core-overview.md (the target), and /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/CLAUDE.md (project context). If the doc makes claims about code/launch/config, spot-check against the real files under /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/src/.
Topic hint: IoT Core thing/policy/endpoint.

ACTUAL CODE/CONFIG ON DISK (ground truth as of this audit):
ROS2 package src/guide_mate_explorer/:
  nodes: bfs_explorer.py, glass_guard.py, depth_lidar_fusion.py, combined_node.py(UNTRACKED/new)
  launch: autonomous_mapping.launch.py, depth_lidar_fusion.launch.py, depth_perception.launch.py, explore.launch.py, combined.launch.py(UNTRACKED/new)
  config: nav2_glass.yaml, oakd_mapping.yaml, slam_fused.yaml(UNTRACKED-ish/new)
NEW untracked C++ package src/guide_mate_perception/:
  src/depth_lidar_fusion_node.cpp, CMakeLists.txt, package.xml, launch/depth_lidar_fusion_cpp.launch.py
rviz/: launch_rviz.sh, turtlebot468_mapping.rviz (tracked)
Root: README.md, CLAUDE.md, .gitignore (tracked); start.sh (gitignored)
Vendored third-party clone (gitignored, untracked, on-disk only): aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/ (full AWS SDK repo with its own .git, ~20 markdowns)
On-disk credentials (gitignored, never committed): Turtlebot-468.cert.pem, Turtlebot-468.private.key, Turtlebot-468.public.key, Turtlebot-468-Policy, root-CA.crt, c9109663-*AccessToken.txt, connect_device_package.zip

Assess the target doc honestly and critically. importance: 'critical'=needed to operate/understand the robot or repo; 'useful'=helpful reference; 'marginal'=nice-to-have/thin; 'obsolete'=stale/superseded/empty-stub. Many tiny section-index README.md files may be 'marginal' boilerplate. Flag any factual drift from the actual code inventory. Be specific in uniqueValue about what is irreplaceably documented here (real hardware findings, IPs, gotchas) vs. generic restatement.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll read the target documentation file and project context, then spot-check claims against the actual code.

content.content:
1	# What AWS IoT Core Is — Building Blocks
2	
3	A managed cloud service that lets **devices** (our TurtleBots) and the **cloud** exchange
4	messages securely, mostly over **MQTT** (a lightweight publish/subscribe protocol). It is a
5	secure message broker plus a set of features layered on top.
6	
7	## Core building blocks
8	
9	### 1. Things & the Registry
10	A "Thing" is the cloud's record of a device — e.g. `Turtlebot-468`. The registry stores
11	metadata, attributes, and group membership. It is bookkeeping only; it does not move data.
12	
13	### 2. Certificates + Policies (security)
14	- Each device authenticates with an **X.509 certificate** (cert + private key) instead of a
15	  password.
16	- The **root CA** lets the device verify it is really talking to AWS (TLS).
17	- An **IoT policy** says *what that certificate may do* — which topics it can publish to /
18	  subscribe to, whether it may connect. Our policy is the narrow quick-start scope: it
19	  allows only `sdk/test/{python,java,js}` topics and a few client IDs. Anything else (tunnel
20	  notify topics, shadows, custom telemetry topics) would require widening the policy.
21	
22	### 3. MQTT message broker (publish / subscribe)
23	Devices **publish** messages to **topics** (e.g. `turtlebot468/telemetry`) and **subscribe**
24	to topics to receive them. The broker routes between publishers and subscribers — they never
25	connect directly. Each AWS account has its own broker endpoint (the **ATS endpoint**,
26	`<your-ats-endpoint>` of the form `xxxx-ats.iot.<region>.amazonaws.com`).
27	
28	**QoS** (quality of service) levels: `0` = at most once (fire-and-forget), `1` = at least
29	once (may duplicate). IoT Core does not support QoS 2.
30	
31	## Features built on top of the broker
32	
33	### 4. Device Shadows
34	A JSON document holding a device's **desired** vs **reported** state. The cloud sets
35	"desired", the device reconciles when it next connects. Good for state you want to set even
36	while the device is offline.
37	
38	### 5. Rules Engine
39	SQL-like rules that react to messages and forward them to other AWS services. This is the
40	main integration path — see [service-integration.md](service-integration.md).
41	
42	### 6. Secure Tunneling
43	Reach a device behind a firewall/NAT **without opening inbound ports** (e.g. SSH into a robot
44	on the lab network). Separate from the MQTT broker. See
45	[secure-tunneling.md](secure-tunneling.md).
46	
47	### 7. Jobs
48	Remote operations dispatched to fleets — firmware updates, config changes, "run this
49	command" — with rollout/success tracking across many devices.
50	
51	### 8. Fleet Provisioning
52	Automates onboarding many devices at scale (cert generation, registration). Relevant if the
53	fleet grows beyond the two lab robots.
54	
55	### 9. Greengrass
56	Runs IoT logic **on the device itself** (edge compute) for local processing when cloud
57	connectivity is poor. Potentially relevant for a robot, where navigation must not depend on
58	cloud latency.
59	
60	## How our files map to these concepts
61	| File on the Pi (gitignored) | IoT Core concept |
62	|---|---|
63	| `Turtlebot-468.cert.pem` / `.private.key` | Device identity (X.509 cert) |
64	| `root-CA.crt` | Verifies AWS's identity (TLS) |
65	| `Turtlebot-468-Policy` | What the device is allowed to do |
66	| `aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/` | Client library to speak MQTT |
67	| `start.sh` | Runs the pub/sub MQTT sample (`sdk/test/python`) |
68	| `*AccessToken.txt` | Secure Tunneling source/destination tokens |
69	
70	## Mental model
71	IoT Core itself is mostly the **MQTT broker + security layer**. It does not store or analyze
72	data on its own — the **Rules Engine routes** messages outward and **Lambda is the glue**.
73	Device↔broker auth uses **X.509 certs + IoT policy**; AWS-internal compute (Lambda, EC2)
74	uses **IAM** instead.
75

content.content:
1	# AWS IoT Core — Overview
2	
3	How AWS IoT Core fits the cs7980 guide-robot project, and what we set up on robot **468**.
4	
5	> ⚠️ **Security:** none of the device credentials live in this repo. The certificate,
6	> private key, root CA, IoT policy, tunnel access tokens, and the AWS "connect a device"
7	> package are **gitignored** (see the repo `.gitignore`). Docs below use placeholders such
8	> as `<account-id>`, `<your-ats-endpoint>`, and `<region>`. The real values for our setup
9	> are in Claude memory (`aws-iot-setup`), not here.
10	
11	## TL;DR
12	- **IoT Core = a managed MQTT message broker + a device security layer**, plus features
13	  built on top (shadows, rules, jobs, secure tunneling).
14	- It does **not** store or analyze data itself — the **Rules Engine** routes messages out
15	  to other AWS services, and **Lambda** is the glue for custom logic.
16	- For the robots it is **robot ↔ cloud** plumbing. It does **not** replace the on-robot
17	  ROS 2 / FastDDS networking (that stays node-to-node; see [network docs](../network/README.md)).
18	- We have the quick-connect MQTT sample working and a **Secure Tunnel** opened, but
19	  `localproxy` is **not yet installed**, so the tunnel is not usable end-to-end yet.
20	
21	## Documents
22	- [What IoT Core is — building blocks](iot-core-overview.md) — things, certs/policies, the MQTT broker, shadows, rules, jobs, Greengrass; and how our files map to each.
23	- [Talking to other AWS services + EC2 access](service-integration.md) — the Rules Engine, service-to-device, EventBridge, and the three ways EC2 can reach IoT Core.
24	- [Secure Tunneling — remote SSH to a robot](secure-tunneling.md) — source/destination tokens, `localproxy`, the MQTT auto-delivery path, and why it isn't connected yet.
25	
26	## What we set up (robot 468)
27	| Piece | State |
28	|---|---|
29	| Thing in the registry | `Turtlebot-468` (region `<region>`) |
30	| Device cert + private key | downloaded to the Pi (gitignored) |
31	| IoT policy | quick-start scope — **only `sdk/test/*` topics**; no tunnel/shadow topics yet |
32	| MQTT pub/sub sample | runs via `start.sh` (publishes to `sdk/test/python`) |
33	| Secure Tunnel | opened; source + destination tokens downloaded (gitignored) |
34	| `localproxy` | **not installed** — required to actually use the tunnel |
35	
36	## Where IoT Core could help the project
37	| Use case | Feature |
38	|---|---|
39	| SSH into a robot behind the lab firewall, no port-forwarding | **Secure Tunneling** |
40	| Push code/config to both 468 and 436 | **Jobs** |
41	| Stream battery/status telemetry to a dashboard or alert | **Rules Engine → SNS / DynamoDB / Timestream** |
42	| Send a command to a robot from a backend | **publish to an MQTT topic** (Lambda/EC2 via IAM) |
43	
44	See the per-topic docs for detail.
45

input.command: ls -la /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/start.sh 2>/dev/null && echo "---START.SH---" && cat /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/start.sh 2>/dev/null | head -60; echo "=== AccessToken sample (first line lengths only, NOT contents) ==="; wc -c /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/c9109663-*AccessToken.txt
input.description: Read policy, README, start.sh, token file sizes

content.content:
-rwxrwxr-x 1 ubuntu ubuntu 1347 Jun 20  2026 /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/start.sh
---START.SH---
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# stop script on error
set -e

# Check for python 3
if ! python3 --version &> /dev/null; then
  printf "\nERROR: python3 must be installed.\n"
  exit 1
fi

# Check to see if root CA file exists, download if not
if [ ! -f ./root-CA.crt ]; then
  printf "\nDownloading AWS IoT Root CA certificate from AWS...\n"
  curl https://www.amazontrust.com/repository/AmazonRootCA1.pem > root-CA.crt
fi

# Check to see if AWS Device SDK for Python exists, download if not
if [ ! -d ./aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2 ]; then
  printf "\nCloning the AWS SDK...\n"
  git clone https://github.com/aws/aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2.git --recursive
fi

# Check to see if AWS Device SDK for Python is already installed, install if not
if ! python3 -c "import awsiot" &> /dev/null; then
  printf "\nInstalling AWS SDK...\n"
  python3 -m pip install ./aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2
  result=$?
  if [ $result -ne 0 ]; then
    printf "\nERROR: Failed to install SDK.\n"
    exit $result
  fi
fi

# run pub/sub sample app using certificates downloaded in package
printf "\nRunning pub/sub sample application...\n"
python3 aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/samples/mqtt/mqtt5_x509.py --endpoint aqc6y1ij55nsq-ats.iot.us-west-2.amazonaws.com --cert Turtlebot-468.cert.pem --key Turtlebot-468.private.key --client_id basicPubSub --topic sdk/test/python --count 0=== AccessToken sample (first line lengths only, NOT contents) ===
 600 /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/c9109663-destinationAccessToken.txt
 600 /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/c9109663-sourceAccessToken.txt
1200 total

content.text:
All claims verified. Let me cross-check the specific factual claims in the target doc against ground truth:

- Policy claims `sdk/test/{python,java,js}` topics and a few client IDs → matches policy file exactly (java/python/js topics; clients `sdk-java`, `basicPubSub`, `sdk-nodejs-*`).
- Account ID `852373397000`, region `us-west-2`, endpoint `aqc6y1ij55nsq-ats.iot.us-west-2.amazonaws.com` → correctly abstracted to placeholders in the doc (security note holds).
- `start.sh` runs pub/sub MQTT sample on `sdk/test/python` with client_id `basicPubSub` → matches.
- File-to-concept mapping table → all files exist on disk as claimed.
- QoS 2 not supported by IoT Core → correct.

The doc makes no claims about the ROS2 package code (bfs_explorer, depth_lidar_fusion, etc.), so there's no drift against the `src/` inventory. It is purely conceptual AWS IoT documentation with an accurate file-mapping table.

input.purpose: Conceptual primer on AWS IoT Core building blocks (things, certs/policies, MQTT broker, shadows, rules, jobs, Greengrass) and how the robot 468 credential files map to each concept.
input.uniqueValue: The irreplaceable content is the project-specific file-to-IoT-concept mapping table (lines 60-68) tying each gitignored on-disk artifact to its role, and the precise policy-scope statement that the cert is limited to sdk/test/* quick-start topics so any real telemetry/tunnel/shadow use requires widening the policy. The rest (things, certs, broker, shadows, rules, jobs, fleet provisioning, Greengrass, QoS levels) is generic AWS IoT Core explanation reproducible from AWS docs.
input.recommendationDetail: Keep as-is. It is the conceptual anchor of the docs/aws-iot/ set and is linked from the section README. Optional trim: the generic feature descriptions (Shadows/Rules/Jobs/Fleet Provisioning/Greengrass, lines 33-58) could be condensed since they restate AWS docs, but the project-specific mapping table and policy-scope note justify keeping the file. No factual corrections needed.
input.summary: A short (~75-line) conceptual overview of AWS IoT Core for the guide-robot project. It explains things/registry, X.509 certs + IoT policies, the MQTT broker and ATS endpoint, QoS, and higher-level features (shadows, rules, jobs, fleet provisioning, Greengrass), then maps the robot 468 on-disk credential files to each concept and gives a mental model (IoT Core = broker + security; Rules Engine routes out; Lambda is glue; IAM for AWS-internal compute). Makes no claims about the ROS2 package code, so there is no drift against the src/ inventory; the few project-specific claims (policy scope, file mapping, start.sh behavior) all check out against disk.
qualityIssues: Sections 4-9 (Shadows through Greengrass) are generic AWS-docs restatement with little project-specific value beyond a one-line 'potentially relevant' note each.
Overlaps the section README.md's TL;DR and 'What we set up' table on the same policy-scope and broker-vs-storage points.

content.text:
Audit complete. Summary of findings for `/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/docs/aws-iot/iot-core-overview.md`:

**Verdict: keep, importance=useful, freshness=accurate, no contradictions.**

Spot-checks against ground truth all passed:
- The policy-scope claim (line 18-20: "only `sdk/test/{python,java,js}` topics and a few client IDs") matches `/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/Turtlebot-468-Policy` exactly — Publish/Receive/Subscribe limited to `sdk/test/java|python|js`, Connect limited to clients `sdk-java`, `basicPubSub`, `sdk-nodejs-*`.
- The file-to-concept mapping table (lines 60-68) lists `Turtlebot-468.cert.pem`, `.private.key`, `root-CA.crt`, `Turtlebot-468-Policy`, `aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/`, `start.sh`, `*AccessToken.txt` — all present on disk.
- The `start.sh` claim ("runs the pub/sub MQTT sample → `sdk/test/python`") matches the actual script, which invokes `mqtt5_x509.py ... --client_id basicPubSub --topic sdk/test/python`.
- Security boundary holds: real account ID (`852373397000`), region (`us-west-2`), and endpoint (`aqc6y1ij55nsq-ats...`) live in the gitignored files; the doc correctly uses `<account-id>`/`<region>`/`<your-ats-endpoint>` placeholders.

No drift against the `src/` code inventory because the doc makes zero claims about the ROS2 package (it is pure AWS conceptual material). 

Irreplaceable content: the project-specific file-to-IoT-concept mapping table and the explicit note that the policy is quick-start-scoped so telemetry/tunnel/shadow use would require widening it. Everything else (broker, shadows, rules, jobs, fleet provisioning, Greengrass, QoS) is generic AWS-docs restatement. Minor redundancy with the section `README.md` TL;DR/setup table.
